one_line_takeaway: A benchmark-grounded comparison of six smart-contract languages showing that accounting model and programming style (account-based/procedural vs. UTXO/approval) largely dictate both which vulnerability classes are possible and how verbose contracts become, with Move's linear types the strongest asset-safety discipline reviewed.
key_finding: Language design choices, more than typing alone, determine which vulnerability classes are even possible: e.g. UTXO (Cardano/Aiken) and Aptos/Move rule out reentrancy by design (no meaningful contract calls / no mutual recursion), UTXO has no transaction-ordering dependence, and Move's linear types statically forbid duplication/loss of assets (mitigating double-spending). These safety gains trade off against usability and verbosity: measured over 10 use cases with implementations in all languages, average LoC was Solidity 34, SmartPy 34, Move 51, PyTeal 88, Aiken 140, and Anchor/Solana 144 -- i.e. the UTXO (Aiken) and Solana (Anchor) implementations were ~4x more verbose than Solidity/SmartPy. The authors conclude (Lesson #4) that the procedural style is overall the most practical, and that assets deserve dedicated type-level treatment (Lesson #2), with Move's linear types the strictest form reviewed.
core_contribution: A hands-on comparative analysis of six mainstream smart-contract languages, each on its own blockchain: Solidity/Ethereum, Rust(+Anchor)/Solana, Aiken/Cardano, (Py)TEAL/Algorand, Move/Aptos, and SmartPy/Tezos. The paper's two conceptual contributions are (i) a categorization of contract languages along two axes -- the accounting model (account-based vs. UTXO) and the induced programming style (procedural vs. approval, with TEAL/PyTeal a hybrid) -- viewing the blockchain at the 'contract layer' as an asset-exchange state machine; and (ii) an original open-source benchmark, 'Rosetta Smart Contracts', that implements a common set of DApp use cases across all six languages, used to draw grounded conclusions about verbosity, readability, security, tooling, on/off-chain interaction, and fees. Note this is a comparison AMONG the six languages themselves; it is not proposing a new tool or defense.
threat_model: The paper is a comparative survey, not an attack/defense contribution, so it defines no single adversary. Section 4.4 (and 4.8) instead maps how language/platform design choices give rise to specific vulnerability CLASSES: reentrancy in Solidity/Ethereum (from callee-has-caller-reference + unbounded nested calls + implicitly-updated ETH balance); missing signer-verification and missing ownership-verification checks in Solana's stateless model, where a caller supplies the accounts a contract reads/writes (illustrated on the stateless Bet contract, where omitting the signer check lets a malicious player bypass the oracle and declare itself winner); transaction-ordering dependence / front-running / MEV in account-based models with unfair ordering; forgotten field-checks in the approval style (Aiken, and PyTeal smart signatures) letting an adversary set an arbitrary next state; gas/fee-based griefing and DoS in Ethereum/Tezos (e.g. growing a map until iteration exceeds the gas limit, freezing funds). Attacks are attributed to design, not to a specific attacker capability model.
limitations: Stated/evident: (1) verbosity is a 'rough' proxy and readability is assessed qualitatively, as the authors acknowledge no widely accepted readability metric exists; (2) one representative language is chosen per platform (Solidity not Vyper, Aiken not Plutus, SmartPy not LIGO), and the authors assert -- but do not exhaustively demonstrate -- that results generalize to alternatives on the same platform; (3) scope is restricted to permissionless blockchains and Turing-complete languages, explicitly excluding Bitcoin script, Pact, and the unmaintained Liquidity, and excluding cross-chain interaction; (4) Algorand is called the hardest to assess because its contract model changed repeatedly; (5) the security discussion is a qualitative mapping of design-to-vulnerability, not an empirical vulnerability study or formal analysis; (6) real-world loss figures (e.g. the Wormhole/Solana $320M+ loss) are cited from prior work as illustrations, not measured by this paper.

datasets_used: Rosetta Smart Contracts benchmark (authors' own, open-source): 21 use cases implemented across 6 languages, 151 source files, ~900KB, ~18K LoC

Use cases include Bet, Simple transfer, HTLC, Escrow, Auction, Crowdfund, Vault, Vesting, Storage, Simple wallet, Price bet, Payment splitter, Lottery, Constant-product AMM, Upgradeable proxy, Factory, Decentralized identity, Editable NFT, Anonymous data, Atomic transactions, Token transfer
quantitative_results: Average LoC over 10 fully-implemented use cases: Solidity/Ethereum 34, Anchor/Solana 144, Aiken/Cardano 140, PyTeal/Algorand 88, Move/Aptos 51, SmartPy/Tezos 34
Bet use case LoC: Solidity 39, Anchor/Solana 137, Aiken 158, PyTeal 110, Move 62, SmartPy 53
Simple wallet LoC: Solidity 47, Anchor 183, Aiken 169, PyTeal 87, Move 108, SmartPy 47 (Move's highest-LoC case)
Storage use case LoC: Solidity 11, Anchor 82, Aiken 75, PyTeal 32, Move 23, SmartPy 18
Benchmark size: 21 use cases, 151 files, ~900KB, ~18K LoC
Anchor framework roughly doubles Solana deployment fees vs pure Rust

Platform storage/size constraints cited: Algorand contract storage limited to ~8KB across max 64 key-value pairs; a single call can read at most 8 boxes; Cardano transaction hard size limit 16KB; Solana transaction size ~1KB
Cited (not measured by this paper): Solana Wormhole attack caused a loss of more than $320 million, illustrating the risk of omitted account validation in the stateless model
baselines_compared: The six languages are compared against each other (Solidity/Ethereum, Rust+Anchor/Solana, Aiken/Cardano, (Py)TEAL/Algorand, Move/Aptos, SmartPy/Tezos)
Positioned against prior literature-based surveys Sharma et al. and Zheng et al., whose comparisons rely on existing literature rather than hands-on implementation
Positioned against prior usability/security comparisons Voloder et al. (5 platforms, developer perspective) and Parizi et al. (Solidity, Pact/Kadena, Liquidity/Tezos)
Extends a prior account-based vs. UTXO comparison (Solidity vs. Plutus token use case) by covering many use cases across six languages
